def: The difference in electric potential between two points in a circuit.
ex: The {{voltage}} across the resistor was measured at twelve volts.

def: The rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor.
ex: A large {{current}} flowing through the wire caused it to overheat.

def: The opposition a material offers to the flow of electric current.
ex: Copper has a low {{resistance}}, making it an excellent conductor.

def: The principle that current equals voltage divided by resistance.
ex: Using {{Ohm's law}}, we calculated the current in the circuit.

def: A component that limits or regulates the flow of electric current.
ex: The {{resistor}} reduced the current to a safe level.

def: A device that stores electrical energy in an electric field.
ex: The {{capacitor}} smoothed out the ripples in the power supply.

def: A coil that stores energy in a magnetic field when current flows.
ex: The {{inductor}} opposed sudden changes in the circuit current.

def: A component that allows current to flow in one direction only.
ex: The {{diode}} prevented current from flowing backwards into the source.

def: A semiconductor device used to amplify or switch electronic signals.
ex: The {{transistor}} acted as a switch controlling the motor current.

def: A closed path through which electric current can flow.
ex: The engineer closed the {{circuit}} and the lamp lit up.

def: A circuit where components are connected end to end sequentially.
ex: In a {{series circuit}}, the same current passes through every component.

def: A circuit where components share the same two connection nodes.
ex: In a {{parallel circuit}}, each branch receives the full supply voltage.

def: Electric current that periodically reverses its direction of flow.
ex: Domestic mains supply uses {{alternating current}} at fifty hertz.

def: Electric current that flows steadily in one single direction.
ex: A battery delivers {{direct current}} to the electronic circuit.

def: The total opposition to alternating current, combining resistance and reactance.
ex: The speaker's {{impedance}} must match the amplifier output.

def: The ability of a component to store electric charge per volt.
ex: The {{capacitance}} of this component is measured in microfarads.

def: The property of a coil that opposes changes in current flow.
ex: High {{inductance}} in the coil delayed the rise of current.

def: The number of cycles of a periodic signal per second.
ex: The mains {{frequency}} in Europe is standardised at fifty hertz.

def: The shape of a signal shown as amplitude over time.
ex: The oscilloscope displayed a clean sinusoidal {{waveform}}.

def: A material that allows electric current to flow easily through it.
ex: Copper is widely used as a {{conductor}} in electrical wiring.

def: A material that strongly resists the flow of electric current.
ex: Rubber is an excellent {{insulator}} used to coat cables.

def: A fundamental property of matter causing electrical force interactions.
ex: The capacitor stored a significant {{electric charge}} on its plates.

def: The energy supplied by a source per unit of charge.
ex: The battery's {{electromotive force}} drives current around the circuit.

def: An unintended low-resistance path causing excessive current flow.
ex: A {{short circuit}} in the wiring tripped the main breaker.

def: Connecting equipment to the ground for electrical safety protection.
ex: Proper {{earthing}} protects users from dangerous fault currents.

def: The rate at which electrical energy is transferred or consumed.
ex: The lamp consumes sixty watts of {{power}} when operating.

def: The capacity to do work, measured in joules or kilowatt-hours.
ex: The meter records the electrical {{energy}} used each month.

def: The SI unit of power, equal to one joule per second.
ex: This heater is rated at two thousand {{watt}} output.

def: The SI unit of electric current, measuring charge flow rate.
ex: The fuse is rated to handle five {{ampere}} of current.

def: The SI unit of electric potential difference and electromotive force.
ex: A standard battery cell supplies about one and a half {{volt}}.

def: The SI unit of electrical resistance and impedance.
ex: The resistor has a value of one thousand {{ohm}}.

def: The SI unit of capacitance, one coulomb per volt.
ex: Most practical capacitors are rated in fractions of a {{farad}}.

def: The SI unit of inductance, one weber per ampere.
ex: The coil's inductance was measured at two {{henry}}.

def: The SI unit of electric charge transported by steady current.
ex: One {{coulomb}} equals the charge moved by one ampere in a second.

def: The SI unit of frequency, equal to one cycle per second.
ex: The oscillator generates a signal at fifty {{hertz}}.

def: A device or component that consumes electrical power in a circuit.
ex: The motor acts as the main {{load}} on the supply.

def: A point where a conductor connects to a component or device.
ex: Connect the red wire to the positive {{terminal}} of the battery.

def: A point where two or more circuit elements connect together.
ex: Kirchhoff's current law applies to every {{node}} in the network.

def: A reference point of zero voltage in an electrical system.
ex: All signal voltages are measured relative to {{ground}}.

def: A device converting stored chemical energy into electrical energy.
ex: The {{battery}} supplied direct current to the portable device.

def: A single unit that generates voltage through chemical reactions.
ex: Several cells connected in series form a complete {{cell}} battery.

def: A device that opens or closes an electrical circuit.
ex: Flipping the {{switch}} completed the circuit and started the fan.

def: A safety device that melts to break a circuit during overload.
ex: The {{fuse}} blew, protecting the appliance from excessive current.

def: An automatic switch that interrupts current during a fault condition.
ex: The {{circuit breaker}} tripped when the load exceeded its rating.

def: A device that changes alternating voltage using electromagnetic induction.
ex: The {{transformer}} stepped the voltage down for the household supply.

def: The opposition to alternating current caused by capacitance or inductance.
ex: Inductive {{reactance}} increases as the signal frequency rises.

def: The ease with which current flows, the inverse of resistance.
ex: A high {{conductance}} means the material passes current readily.

def: The maximum displacement of a signal from its equilibrium value.
ex: The signal's {{amplitude}} determines the loudness of the output.

def: The position of a point within a wave's periodic cycle.
ex: The current lags the voltage by a ninety degree {{phase}}.

def: The distance between successive corresponding points of a wave.
ex: The antenna length is related to the signal {{wavelength}}.

def: A material with conductivity between a conductor and an insulator.
ex: Silicon is the most common {{semiconductor}} used in electronics.

def: A circuit with a break preventing any current from flowing.
ex: A broken wire created an {{open circuit}} and the lamp failed.

def: A complete circuit that allows current to flow continuously.
ex: Only in a {{closed circuit}} does current reach the load.

def: The property of having positive and negative electrical terminals.
ex: Reversing the {{polarity}} of the diode blocked the current.

def: A conductor through which current enters or leaves a device.
ex: The positive {{electrode}} of the cell is called the anode.

def: The electrode through which conventional current enters a device.
ex: Current flows into the diode through its {{anode}} terminal.

def: The electrode through which conventional current leaves a device.
ex: Electrons are emitted from the heated {{cathode}} of the tube.

def: A device that provides electrical energy to a circuit or system.
ex: The regulated {{power supply}} delivered a stable five volts.

def: A device that converts alternating current into direct current.
ex: The bridge {{rectifier}} converted the AC input to pulsating DC.

def: A device that increases the amplitude of an electrical signal.
ex: The audio {{amplifier}} boosted the weak signal to drive speakers.

def: A circuit that produces a repetitive alternating output signal.
ex: The crystal {{oscillator}} generates a precise clock frequency.

def: The condition where a circuit responds strongly at a specific frequency.
ex: At {{resonance}}, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel out.

def: The ratio of real power to apparent power in AC systems.
ex: A low {{power factor}} increases losses in the distribution network.

def: The reduction in voltage across a component carrying current.
ex: The {{voltage drop}} across the long cable reduced the load voltage.

def: The generation of voltage by a changing magnetic field.
ex: {{Electromagnetic induction}} is the operating principle behind every generator.

def: A region where magnetic forces act on moving electric charges.
ex: Current in the coil creates a {{magnetic field}} around it.

def: A region where electric forces act on charged particles.
ex: The charged plates set up a uniform {{electric field}} between them.

def: An insulating material that becomes polarised in an electric field.
ex: The capacitor uses a ceramic {{dielectric}} between its plates.

def: A material property quantifying its opposition to current flow.
ex: Silver has the lowest {{resistivity}} of all common metals.

def: A rule governing current and voltage relationships in electrical networks.
ex: We applied {{Kirchhoff's law}} to solve the complex circuit.

def: A smooth periodic waveform describing a pure oscillation.
ex: The generator produced a clean {{sine wave}} at the output.

def: The fraction of one period a signal stays active.
ex: Increasing the {{duty cycle}} raised the average motor voltage.

def: The amount of electrical power expressed in watts.
ex: Choose a bulb with a lower {{wattage}} to save energy.

def: A machine that converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.
ex: The backup {{generator}} started automatically when the mains supply failed.

def: A machine that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation.
ex: The {{electric motor}} drives the conveyor belt at constant speed.

def: A generator that produces alternating current from mechanical energy.
ex: The car's {{alternator}} charges the battery while the engine runs.

def: A conductor that carries electrical power over long distances.
ex: High-voltage {{transmission line}} towers span the entire valley.

def: A facility that transforms voltage and distributes electrical power.
ex: The new {{substation}} supplies power to the entire industrial park.

def: An interconnected network for delivering electricity from producers to consumers.
ex: The regional {{power grid}} balances supply and demand in real time.

def: A power system using three alternating currents offset in phase.
ex: Heavy machinery requires a {{three-phase supply}} for smooth operation.

def: A metallic bar that conducts and distributes current within switchgear.
ex: The copper {{busbar}} carries current to several distribution circuits.

def: An electrically operated switch that controls a circuit remotely.
ex: A protective {{relay}} isolates the faulty section of the line.

def: Combined switches and fuses used to control and protect equipment.
ex: The {{switchgear}} panel houses breakers, relays, and isolators.

def: A device that converts direct current into alternating current.
ex: The solar {{inverter}} feeds AC power back into the grid.

def: A panel that divides power into separate protected circuits.
ex: Each apartment has its own {{distribution board}} with labelled breakers.

def: Deliberate disconnection of supply to prevent grid overload.
ex: Scheduled {{load shedding}} reduced demand during the heat wave.

def: Keeping output voltage stable despite changing load conditions.
ex: Good {{voltage regulation}} keeps appliance voltage within safe limits.

def: The rotating part of an electrical machine or generator.
ex: The {{rotor}} spins inside the stator to induce voltage.

def: The stationary part of an electrical machine surrounding the rotor.
ex: The {{stator}} windings carry the current that produces the field.

def: The winding where voltage is induced in a machine.
ex: Current flowing through the {{armature}} produces the driving torque.

def: A rotary switch that reverses current direction in DC machines.
ex: The {{commutator}} and brushes maintain steady current in the motor.

def: A current greater than the rated capacity of equipment.
ex: The fuse blew because of a sustained {{overload}} on the circuit.

def: A connection to earth that protects people from electric shock.
ex: A proper {{earthing system}} channels fault current safely to ground.

def: A switch used to isolate equipment for safe maintenance.
ex: The engineer opened the {{isolator}} before servicing the transformer.

def: A coil of wire wound to produce a magnetic field.
ex: A short in the {{winding}} caused the motor to overheat.

def: The supply of current to create a generator's magnetic field.
ex: Loss of {{excitation}} causes the generator voltage to collapse.

def: A machine whose rotor turns in step with the supply frequency.
ex: The {{synchronous machine}} runs at constant speed regardless of load.

def: A motor where rotor current is induced by the stator field.
ex: The rugged {{induction motor}} drives the industrial water pump.

def: The rotational force produced by an electric motor.
ex: The motor delivers high starting {{torque}} under heavy load.

def: The angular difference between voltage and current waveforms.
ex: A large {{phase angle}} indicates a strongly inductive load.

def: A unit of energy equal to one kilowatt used for one hour.
ex: The meter recorded three hundred {{kilowatt-hour}} of consumption.

def: The product of voltage and current, measured in volt-amperes.
ex: The transformer's {{apparent power}} rating is five hundred kilovolt-amperes.

def: Power that oscillates between source and load without doing work.
ex: Excess {{reactive power}} lowers the efficiency of the network.

def: A brief, sharp rise in voltage or current in a system.
ex: A lightning {{surge}} damaged several sensitive electronic devices.

def: A voltage or current at a multiple of the fundamental frequency.
ex: Nonlinear loads inject {{harmonic}} distortion into the supply.

def: A line carrying power from a substation to distribution points.
ex: A fault on the {{feeder}} cut supply to the whole district.

def: Connecting equipment to earth for safety and stable reference.
ex: Proper {{grounding}} prevents dangerous voltages on metal casings.

def: The resistance of insulation against current leakage to earth.
ex: Low {{insulation resistance}} indicates moisture inside the cable.

def: Operating with no external electrical load connected to the machine.
ex: The transformer's {{no-load}} losses come mainly from the core.

def: Operating at the maximum rated load of the equipment.
ex: At {{full-load}} the motor draws its rated current continuously.

def: The ratio of useful output power to total input power.
ex: The modern motor reaches an {{efficiency}} of ninety-five percent.

def: The difference between synchronous and actual rotor speed in a motor.
ex: Under heavy load the {{slip}} of the induction motor increases.

def: A carbon contact that transfers current to a rotating commutator.
ex: A worn {{brush}} caused sparking at the motor commutator.

def: A magnetic region of a machine where field lines concentrate.
ex: A four-{{pole}} motor runs at half the speed of a two-pole one.

def: The small space between the rotor and stator of a machine.
ex: A uniform {{air gap}} keeps the magnetic field balanced.

def: An earthed conductor that guards against electric shock hazards.
ex: The {{protective earth}} conductor connects to every exposed metal part.

def: The rate charged for a unit of electrical energy consumed.
ex: The night {{tariff}} makes off-peak electricity much cheaper.

def: An insulated conductor used to carry electrical power or signals.
ex: The underground {{cable}} feeds power to the residential block.

def: An electrical conductor suspended above ground on towers or poles.
ex: Strong wind swung the {{overhead line}} between the pylons.

def: A tall steel tower that supports high-voltage transmission lines.
ex: Each {{pylon}} carries several bundled high-voltage conductors.

def: A device that protects equipment from transient overvoltage surges.
ex: The {{surge arrester}} diverts lightning current safely to earth.

def: The ratio of average load to peak load over time.
ex: A high {{load factor}} means the plant is used efficiently.

def: The maximum output a device can deliver continuously and safely.
ex: The generator operated below its {{rated capacity}} all afternoon.

def: An industrial facility that generates electrical power at scale.
ex: The hydroelectric {{power plant}} supplies the northern region.

def: Distributing electrical demand evenly across phases or sources.
ex: Careful {{load balancing}} prevents overloading a single phase.

def: The return conductor that carries current back to the source.
ex: The {{neutral}} wire completes the circuit in a single-phase system.

def: A compact integrated circuit containing a processor, memory, and input/output peripherals.
ex: The robot's motors are driven by a small {{microcontroller}}.

def: A set of electronic circuits fabricated together on a single semiconductor chip.
ex: An {{integrated circuit}} can hold billions of transistors.

def: A high-gain voltage amplifier with differential inputs used in analogue circuits.
ex: An {{operational amplifier}} is configured to sum two input voltages.

def: A varying quantity that carries information from one point to another.
ex: The sensor converts pressure into an electrical {{signal}}.

def: The process of varying a carrier wave to encode information for transmission.
ex: Frequency {{modulation}} keeps radio broadcasts clear of static.

def: The recovery of the original information signal from a modulated carrier wave.
ex: The receiver performs {{demodulation}} to extract the audio.

def: A device that detects a physical quantity and converts it into a signal.
ex: A temperature {{sensor}} triggers the cooling fan.

def: A component that converts a control signal into physical motion or action.
ex: The valve {{actuator}} opens the pipe automatically.

def: Returning part of an output signal to the input to control a system.
ex: Negative {{feedback}} stabilises the amplifier's gain.

def: A set of devices that manages the behaviour of other systems or processes.
ex: A closed-loop {{control system}} regulates the motor speed.

def: A basic digital circuit that performs a logical operation on binary inputs.
ex: An AND {{logic gate}} outputs one only when both inputs are one.

def: A digital circuit that stores one bit of data as a stable state.
ex: A {{flip-flop}} holds its output until the clock changes.

def: A continuous signal whose value varies smoothly over time.
ex: A microphone produces an {{analogue signal}} from sound waves.

def: A signal that represents information as discrete levels or binary values.
ex: The converter turns the voltage into a {{digital signal}}.

def: A circuit that passes certain frequencies while blocking others.
ex: A low-pass {{filter}} removes high-frequency noise.

def: The ratio of output signal amplitude to input signal amplitude in a circuit.
ex: Increasing the {{gain}} makes the output louder.

def: The range of frequencies that a system or channel can effectively handle.
ex: A wider {{bandwidth}} allows faster data transmission.

def: A board that mechanically supports and electrically connects electronic components.
ex: Components are soldered onto the {{printed circuit board}}.

def: A reusable board for building temporary circuits without soldering.
ex: He tested the design on a {{breadboard}} first.

def: A device that converts one form of energy into another.
ex: A microphone is a {{transducer}} that turns sound into voltage.

def: A technique controlling power by varying the width of digital pulses.
ex: {{Pulse width modulation}} dims the LED without wasting energy.

def: Unwanted random disturbance that corrupts or masks a useful signal.
ex: Shielding reduces electrical {{noise}} in the cable.

def: An integrated circuit that performs the computing functions of a computer.
ex: The laptop's {{microprocessor}} executes millions of instructions per second.

def: A circuit that converts a continuous analogue signal into digital values.
ex: The {{analogue-to-digital converter}} samples the sensor voltage.

def: A circuit that converts digital data into a continuous analogue signal.
ex: The {{digital-to-analogue converter}} recreates the audio waveform.

def: A periodic pulse that synchronises operations in a digital circuit.
ex: Every register updates on the rising {{clock signal}}.

def: A circuit that selects one of several inputs and forwards it to output.
ex: A {{multiplexer}} routes many sensors to one line.

def: A small fast storage element that holds bits inside a processor.
ex: The result is stored in a temporary {{register}}.

def: A constant equal to an amplifier's gain multiplied by its bandwidth.
ex: A high {{gain-bandwidth product}} suits fast op-amp designs.

def: A variable resistor used to adjust voltage by turning a knob.
ex: The volume {{potentiometer}} sets the output level.

def: A circuit that compares two voltages and outputs which is larger.
ex: The {{comparator}} switches high when the input exceeds the reference.

def: A device that converts information into a coded electrical form.
ex: A rotary {{encoder}} reports the shaft's angular position.

def: A circuit that converts coded input into a recognisable output.
ex: The {{decoder}} activates one output line per input code.

def: The gradual reduction of a signal's strength as it travels.
ex: Long cables cause noticeable {{attenuation}} of the signal.

def: Measuring a continuous signal at regular time intervals.
ex: A higher {{sampling}} rate captures more detail.

def: Mapping a continuous range of values onto discrete digital levels.
ex: Coarse {{quantisation}} introduces rounding error in the signal.

def: A tiny wafer of semiconductor carrying an integrated electronic circuit.
ex: A single {{microchip}} controls the whole device.

def: A fusible metal alloy used to join electronic components electrically.
ex: He applied {{solder}} to fix the joint.

def: A short wire that connects two points on a circuit board.
ex: Place a {{jumper}} to close the connection.

def: The delay between a signal being sent and its response.
ex: Low {{latency}} keeps the control loop responsive.

def: The signal level at which a circuit changes its output state.
ex: The gate switches when input crosses the {{threshold}}.

def: The control terminal of a field-effect transistor governing current flow.
ex: A voltage on the {{gate}} turns the transistor on.

def: A fixed voltage or current setting a device's operating point.
ex: Correct {{bias}} keeps the transistor in its active region.

def: An instrument that displays how a signal's voltage varies over time.
ex: The engineer viewed the pulse on an {{oscilloscope}}.

def: A diagram showing components and connections of an electronic circuit.
ex: Read the {{schematic}} before wiring the board.

def: Software stored permanently on a device to control its hardware.
ex: Updating the {{firmware}} fixed the sensor bug.

def: A path returning output back to the input to regulate a system.
ex: The {{feedback loop}} keeps the temperature constant.

def: How much gain a control system can gain before becoming unstable.
ex: A small {{gain margin}} warns of possible oscillation.

def: The condition of a system after transients have died away.
ex: In {{steady state}} the output stays constant.

def: A short-lived change in a signal before it settles down.
ex: A voltage {{transient}} can damage sensitive chips.

def: Unwanted coupling of a signal from one channel into another.
ex: Poor layout causes {{crosstalk}} between adjacent tracks.

def: A resistor that holds a logic line high when otherwise undriven.
ex: Add a {{pull-up resistor}} to keep the pin high.

def: A metal part that draws heat away from an electronic component.
ex: A {{heat sink}} keeps the processor from overheating.

def: The number of signal samples taken per second by a converter.
ex: A higher {{sample rate}} reproduces music more faithfully.

def: A number expressed using only the two digits zero and one
ex: A {{binary number}} uses base two to represent digital data.

def: The smallest unit of digital information, holding a zero or one
ex: A single {{bit}} stores one binary digit of information.

def: A group of eight bits treated as one unit of memory
ex: One {{byte}} consists of eight consecutive binary digits.

def: A group of four bits, equal to half a byte
ex: A {{nibble}} represents one hexadecimal digit conveniently.

def: A table listing all input combinations and their logic outputs
ex: The {{truth table}} defines the output for every input combination.

def: The mathematics of logic operations on true and false values
ex: Engineers simplify circuits using {{boolean algebra}} identities.

def: Logic whose output depends only on present input values
ex: {{Combinational logic}} produces outputs without any memory of past states.

def: Logic whose output depends on inputs and stored past states
ex: {{Sequential logic}} uses memory elements clocked by timing signals.

def: A bistable circuit that stores one bit until updated
ex: An SR {{latch}} holds a single bit of state.

def: A circuit that counts clock pulses and stores the total
ex: The {{counter}} increments its value on each rising clock edge.

def: A logic circuit that computes the arithmetic sum of binary numbers
ex: The {{adder}} produces a sum and a carry output.

def: An adder that sums two bits without a carry input
ex: A {{half adder}} outputs a sum and carry from two bits.

def: An adder that sums three bits including an incoming carry
ex: A {{full adder}} adds two bits plus a carry-in.

def: The processor block performing arithmetic and logical operations
ex: The {{arithmetic logic unit}} executes additions and bitwise operations.

def: A unique number identifying a location within memory
ex: Each byte is stored at a distinct {{memory address}}.

def: A group of lines carrying data between processor components
ex: The {{data bus}} transfers bytes between the CPU and memory.

def: A set of lines carrying memory addresses from the processor
ex: The {{address bus}} selects which memory location to access.

def: Instructions in binary form executed directly by a processor
ex: The compiler translates the program into {{machine code}}.

def: A signal that halts the processor to service an urgent event
ex: An {{interrupt}} suspends the current task to handle a request.

def: A gate outputting one only when all inputs are one
ex: An {{AND gate}} outputs true only if every input is true.

def: A gate outputting one when at least one input is one
ex: An {{OR gate}} outputs true if any input is true.

def: A gate giving the inverted output of an AND gate
ex: A {{NAND gate}} is a universal building block for logic.

def: A gate giving the inverted output of an OR gate
ex: A {{NOR gate}} outputs true only when all inputs are false.

def: A gate outputting one when its inputs differ from each other
ex: An {{XOR gate}} detects when two input bits are unequal.

def: A register that moves stored bits sideways on each clock pulse
ex: The {{shift register}} converts serial data into parallel form.

def: A processor register holding intermediate arithmetic and logic results
ex: The result of the addition is stored in the {{accumulator}}.

def: A register holding the address of the next instruction
ex: The {{program counter}} advances to fetch the following instruction.

def: The complete collection of operations a processor can execute
ex: Each processor family has its own {{instruction set}}.

def: The part of an instruction specifying the operation to perform
ex: The {{opcode}} tells the processor which action to carry out.

def: A low-level language using mnemonics for machine instructions
ex: Programmers write time-critical routines in {{assembly language}}.

def: Fast memory storing recently used data close to the processor
ex: {{Cache memory}} reduces the time to access frequent data.

def: Volatile memory allowing read and write at any address
ex: Programs load into {{random access memory}} during execution.

def: Non-volatile memory whose contents are fixed and only read
ex: The firmware is stored in {{read-only memory}}.

def: One complete period of the processor's timing signal
ex: Each instruction may require several {{clock cycle}}s to finish.

def: The time for a signal change to pass through a gate
ex: Excessive {{propagation delay}} limits the maximum clock speed.

def: The number of gate inputs one output can reliably drive
ex: The gate's {{fan-out}} limits how many inputs it feeds.

def: The number of inputs a logic gate can accept
ex: A gate with high {{fan-in}} accepts many input signals.

def: A voltage range representing a binary zero or one
ex: A high {{logic level}} typically represents a binary one.

def: A base-sixteen number system using digits zero to F
ex: Memory addresses are often written in {{hexadecimal}} notation.

def: A coding where each decimal digit uses four binary bits
ex: {{Binary-coded decimal}} represents each digit with four bits.

def: An extra bit added to detect errors in transmitted data
ex: The {{parity bit}} makes the number of ones even.

def: A computed value used to verify data integrity
ex: The receiver compares the {{checksum}} to detect transmission errors.

def: A circuit routing one input to one of several outputs
ex: The {{demultiplexer}} sends data to a selected output line.

def: A grid used to simplify boolean logic expressions visually
ex: Engineers minimise logic functions using a {{Karnaugh map}}.

def: A model with a finite number of states and transitions
ex: The controller is designed as a {{finite state machine}}.

def: The interval data must be stable before a clock edge
ex: Data must be valid within the {{setup time}} before clocking.

def: The interval data must stay stable after a clock edge
ex: The signal must remain fixed during the {{hold time}}.

def: A buffer with high, low and high-impedance output states
ex: A {{tri-state buffer}} isolates its output when disabled.

def: A gate that strengthens a signal without changing its logic
ex: The {{buffer}} drives a heavy load without inverting the signal.

def: A register holding the address of the stack's top
ex: The {{stack pointer}} tracks the current top of the stack.

def: The number of bits a processor handles as one unit
ex: A processor with a large {{word length}} handles wider data.

def: A register storing condition flags from recent operations
ex: The {{status register}} records the carry and zero flags.

def: The processor part that directs and coordinates operations
ex: The {{control unit}} decodes instructions and issues control signals.

def: The repeated process of retrieving and running each instruction
ex: The processor repeats the {{fetch-execute cycle}} for every instruction.

def: A technique overlapping instruction stages to raise throughput
ex: The {{pipeline}} processes several instructions at overlapping stages.

def: Activation occurring on a rising or falling clock transition
ex: The flip-flop uses {{edge trigger}}ing on the rising clock.

def: A binary code where successive values differ by one bit
ex: {{Gray code}} reduces errors in rotary position encoders.

def: A comparator with hysteresis producing clean digital transitions
ex: A {{Schmitt trigger}} sharpens a slowly changing input signal.

def: A circuit producing one fixed-length pulse when triggered
ex: A {{monostable}} generates a single timed output pulse.

def: A circuit oscillating continuously between two states without triggering
ex: An {{astable}} multivibrator produces a continuous square wave.

def: Removing spurious rapid transitions from a mechanical switch signal
ex: {{Debouncing}} filters out false pulses from a pressed button.

def: A counter where each stage clocks the next in sequence
ex: A {{ripple counter}} propagates the clock through cascaded stages.

def: A counter whose stages all change on the same clock
ex: A {{synchronous counter}} updates every stage simultaneously.

def: A display forming digits from seven illuminated bar segments
ex: The {{seven-segment display}} shows decimal digits on the panel.

def: A chip whose logic can be reconfigured after manufacture
ex: The design is prototyped on a {{field-programmable gate array}}.

def: Placing hundreds of thousands of transistors on one chip
ex: Modern processors rely on {{very-large-scale integration}} technology.

def: The number of signal symbols transmitted per second
ex: The serial link operates at a {{baud rate}} of nine thousand.

def: Sending data one bit at a time over a line
ex: {{Serial communication}} transmits bits sequentially on a single wire.

def: A method letting devices transfer data without the processor
ex: {{Direct memory access}} moves data blocks without CPU intervention.

def: Memory that loses its contents when power is removed
ex: {{Volatile memory}} loses stored data once power is cut.

def: Memory that retains its contents when power is off
ex: {{Non-volatile memory}} keeps data without a power supply.

def: Non-volatile memory that can be electrically erased and reprogrammed
ex: Firmware updates are written to {{flash memory}}.

def: A timer that resets the system if software stalls
ex: The {{watchdog timer}} restarts the microcontroller after a fault.

def: A method of representing signed integers in binary form
ex: Negative numbers are stored using {{two's complement}} notation.

def: The bit carrying the smallest weight in a binary number
ex: The {{least significant bit}} changes most frequently when counting.

def: The bit carrying the greatest weight in a binary number
ex: The {{most significant bit}} often indicates the number's sign.

def: A result too large for the available number of bits
ex: An {{overflow}} occurs when the sum exceeds the register width.

def: An unstable state when timing constraints are violated
ex: {{Metastability}} can occur when signals cross clock domains.

def: A steady wave that is modulated to carry information across a channel.
ex: The {{carrier wave}} is modulated so that voice signals can be transmitted.

def: A technique that varies a carrier's amplitude in step with the message signal.
ex: AM radio uses {{amplitude modulation}} to broadcast sound over long distances.

def: A method that varies the carrier's frequency according to the input signal.
ex: FM broadcasting relies on {{frequency modulation}} to reduce audible noise.

def: A scheme in which the carrier's phase is varied to encode data.
ex: Digital links often use {{phase modulation}} to carry high data rates reliably.

def: The ratio of desired signal power to background noise power in a system.
ex: A high {{signal-to-noise ratio}} means the message is clear and easy to decode.

def: A device that radiates or captures electromagnetic waves for communication.
ex: The {{antenna}} on the roof receives signals from a distant transmitter.

def: A device that generates and sends modulated signals over a channel.
ex: The radio {{transmitter}} broadcasts programmes across the whole region.

def: A device that captures and demodulates incoming signals into usable form.
ex: A sensitive {{receiver}} can detect very weak signals from space.

def: A hollow structure that guides electromagnetic waves along its length.
ex: A rectangular {{waveguide}} carries microwave energy to the antenna feed.

def: An instrument that displays signal power against frequency for analysis.
ex: Engineers used a {{spectrum analyser}} to inspect the transmitter's output.

def: A mathematical tool converting a time signal into its frequency components.
ex: The {{Fourier transform}} reveals which frequencies dominate the measured signal.

def: A circuit that passes low frequencies and blocks high ones.
ex: A {{low-pass filter}} removes high-frequency hiss from the audio output.

def: A circuit that passes high frequencies and blocks low ones.
ex: A {{high-pass filter}} blocks the slow hum while keeping the treble.

def: A filter that passes a chosen band of frequencies and rejects others.
ex: The receiver's {{band-pass filter}} selects only the wanted radio channel.

def: Any unwanted change in the shape of a signal during processing.
ex: Overdriving the amplifier introduces {{distortion}} into the output signal.

def: Combining several signals for transmission over one shared channel.
ex: Careful {{multiplexing}} lets many phone calls travel down a single fibre.

def: Sending several optical signals on different wavelengths through one fibre.
ex: Optical {{wavelength-division multiplexing}} greatly increases the capacity of long links.

def: Technology that transmits data as light through thin glass fibres.
ex: Modern networks rely on {{fibre optics}} for very fast data transfer.

def: A band of frequencies produced on either side of a modulated carrier.
ex: Filtering out one {{sideband}} saves precious spectrum in radio transmission.

def: The original frequency range of a signal before modulation.
ex: The {{baseband}} audio is shifted upward before it reaches the antenna.

def: The range of frequencies that a filter allows to pass.
ex: Signals inside the {{passband}} reach the output almost unchanged.

def: The range of frequencies that a filter strongly attenuates.
ex: Frequencies in the {{stopband}} are heavily suppressed by the circuit.

def: The frequency at which a filter's response falls to half power.
ex: Above the {{cut-off frequency}}, the low-pass filter sharply reduces the output.

def: The rate at which a filter's gain decreases beyond the cut-off.
ex: A steeper {{roll-off}} gives sharper separation between wanted and unwanted bands.

def: The time delay a signal's envelope experiences passing through a system.
ex: Uneven {{group delay}} can smear pulses and distort digital data.

def: The minimum sampling rate needed to reproduce a signal without loss.
ex: Sampling below the {{Nyquist rate}} causes irreversible aliasing errors.

def: Distortion where high frequencies masquerade as lower ones after undersampling.
ex: Too low a sampling rate produces {{aliasing}} that ruins the signal.

def: The ratio between the largest and smallest signals a system handles.
ex: A wide {{dynamic range}} captures both quiet and loud sounds cleanly.

def: The number of bits transmitted per second over a channel.
ex: A higher {{bit rate}} allows more data to flow each second.

def: The fraction of received bits that are wrong in a transmission.
ex: A low {{bit error rate}} indicates a reliable digital communication link.

def: The maximum error-free data rate a channel can theoretically support.
ex: Shannon's theorem sets the {{channel capacity}} for a given noise level.

def: A scheme combining amplitude and phase changes to carry many bits.
ex: Cable modems use {{quadrature amplitude modulation}} to reach very high data rates.

def: Digital modulation that encodes data as discrete carrier phase states.
ex: Satellite links favour {{phase-shift keying}} for its resistance to noise.

def: Digital modulation that represents bits by switching carrier frequency.
ex: Early modems used {{frequency-shift keying}} to send data over phone lines.

ipa: /p Мls k…Щ Кd ЋМm…Тdj КЋИle…™ Г…Щn/
def: A method of encoding sampled amplitudes as binary numbers.
ex: Digital telephony converts speech to bits using {{pulse-code modulation}}.

def: A technique that spreads a signal over a wide frequency band.
ex: Using {{spread spectrum}} makes transmissions harder to jam or intercept.

def: Rapidly switching the carrier among many frequencies during transmission.
ex: Rapid {{frequency hopping}} improves security by constantly changing the operating channel.

def: Sharing a channel by giving each signal its own time slot.
ex: Digital {{time-division multiplexing}} lets several streams share one line in turn.

def: Sharing a channel by assigning each signal a separate frequency band.
ex: Classic {{frequency-division multiplexing}} places many radio stations on one cable.

def: Unwanted mixing products created when signals pass through a non-linear device.
ex: Strong nearby stations cause {{intermodulation}} that produces spurious tones.

def: A circuit that combines two signals to shift frequency.
ex: The {{mixer}} translates the incoming signal down to an intermediate frequency.

def: An oscillator whose signal is mixed with the input to shift frequency.
ex: Tuning the {{local oscillator}} selects which station the receiver picks up.

def: A fixed frequency to which signals are shifted for easier processing.
ex: The receiver amplifies the {{intermediate frequency}} before demodulating the message.

def: The range of electromagnetic frequencies used for wireless communication.
ex: The {{radio frequency}} signal travels from the tower to your phone.

def: A short-wavelength radio wave used for high-capacity links and radar.
ex: A {{microwave}} link carries data between two distant relay towers.

def: A simple antenna made of two conductive elements fed at the centre.
ex: A half-wave {{dipole antenna}} radiates efficiently at its resonant frequency.

def: The angular width over which an antenna radiates most of its power.
ex: A narrow {{beamwidth}} concentrates the signal toward a distant receiver.

def: An unwanted secondary radiation pattern beside an antenna's main beam.
ex: Strong {{side lobe}} radiation can cause interference in other directions.

def: The orientation of an electromagnetic wave's electric field vector.
ex: Matching the antenna's {{polarisation}} maximises the received signal strength.

def: The way electromagnetic waves travel through space or a medium.
ex: Radio {{propagation}} depends heavily on the state of the ionosphere.

def: The bending of waves around obstacles or through small openings.
ex: Wave {{diffraction}} lets radio signals reach receivers hidden behind hills.

def: Variation in received signal strength caused by changing propagation conditions.
ex: Deep {{fading}} can briefly drop a mobile call in a moving car.

def: Reception of a signal via several paths of different lengths.
ex: Severe {{multipath}} reflections cause echoes that can distort digital reception.

def: A change in observed frequency caused by relative motion.
ex: The {{Doppler shift}} raises the pitch of an approaching source.

def: A shielded cable with a central conductor for carrying high-frequency signals.
ex: A {{coaxial cable}} connects the antenna to the receiver with low loss.

def: The spreading of a pulse because different components travel at different speeds.
ex: Optical {{dispersion}} in long fibres limits how fast data can be sent.

def: A device that converts received light into an electrical signal.
ex: At the fibre's end a {{photodetector}} turns light pulses back into data.

def: A semiconductor device that emits coherent light for optical links.
ex: A {{laser diode}} launches modulated light into the optical fibre.

def: A device that receives, amplifies and retransmits a signal to extend range.
ex: A {{repeater}} boosts the weakened signal so it can travel further.

def: A circuit that corrects frequency-dependent distortion in a channel.
ex: An adaptive {{equaliser}} compensates for the cable's uneven response.

def: A filter designed to maximise signal-to-noise ratio for a known waveform.
ex: The receiver uses a {{matched filter}} to detect the expected pulse.

def: A mathematical operation describing how a system reshapes an input signal.
ex: The output equals the {{convolution}} of the input with the impulse response.

def: A measure of how a signal matches a delayed copy of itself.
ex: Computing {{autocorrelation}} helps detect repeating patterns hidden within noisy data.

def: How a signal's power is distributed across its frequency components.
ex: The {{power spectral density}} shows where most of the energy lies.

def: An efficient algorithm for computing the discrete Fourier transform.
ex: The processor runs a {{fast Fourier transform}} to analyse the spectrum quickly.

def: A system's output when driven by a very short input pulse.
ex: Measuring the {{impulse response}} fully characterises a linear system.

def: A mathematical description relating a system's output to its input.
ex: The filter's {{transfer function}} shows how each frequency is treated.

def: How a system's output amplitude and phase vary with frequency.
ex: A flat {{frequency response}} means every tone is reproduced equally.

def: A logarithmic unit expressing the ratio of two power levels.
ex: The amplifier adds thirty {{decibel}} of gain to the signal.

def: A smooth curve outlining the extremes of a modulated signal.
ex: An AM detector recovers audio by following the signal's {{envelope}}.

def: An unused frequency gap that separates adjacent channels to prevent interference.
ex: A narrow {{guard band}} keeps neighbouring channels from overlapping and interfering.

def: The actual rate of successful data delivery over a channel.
ex: Heavy interference reduced the link's {{throughput}} below its rated speed.

def: Adding redundant bits so a receiver can correct errors without retransmission.
ex: Robust {{forward error correction}} lets the receiver fix bit errors on its own.

def: A wave pattern formed when incident and reflected waves interfere.
ex: A mismatch on the line creates a {{standing wave}} that wastes power.

def: A four-layer semiconductor switch that stays on once triggered by a gate pulse.
ex: The rectifier bridge used a {{thyristor}} to control the output voltage.

def: A power transistor combining fast switching with high current handling for converters.
ex: Each inverter leg was built around a rugged {{IGBT}} module.

def: A voltage-controlled transistor widely used for fast, efficient power switching.
ex: A logic-level {{MOSFET}} drove the low-voltage motor stage.

def: A circuit that changes electrical power from one form to another.
ex: The {{power converter}} stepped the battery voltage up for the motor.

def: A device that converts one direct-current voltage level to another.
ex: A {{DC-DC converter}} regulated the panel output for charging.

def: A step-down converter that produces a lower output voltage than its input.
ex: The {{buck converter}} dropped twelve volts down to five.

def: A step-up converter that produces a higher output voltage than its input.
ex: A {{boost converter}} raised the cell voltage to drive the bus.

def: The rate at which a converter switch turns on and off per second.
ex: Raising the {{switching frequency}} let engineers use smaller inductors.

def: A protective network that suppresses voltage spikes across a switching device.
ex: An RC {{snubber circuit}} tamed the transistor's turn-off overshoot.

def: A diode that provides a safe path for inductive current during switching.
ex: The {{freewheeling diode}} protected the transistor from the coil's back-EMF.

def: An inverter fed from a stiff DC voltage that synthesises AC output.
ex: The drive used a three-phase {{voltage source inverter}} for the motor.

def: An inverter that synchronises solar output to the utility grid.
ex: The {{grid-tie inverter}} fed surplus solar power back to the network.

def: A device converting solar panel DC into usable AC electricity.
ex: The rooftop {{solar inverter}} supplied the household appliances.

def: A semiconductor device that converts sunlight directly into electricity.
ex: Each {{photovoltaic cell}} generated a small voltage under sunlight.

def: An assembly of photovoltaic cells that generates power from sunlight.
ex: They mounted the {{solar panel}} on the south-facing roof.

def: A group of connected solar panels forming a larger generating unit.
ex: The {{photovoltaic array}} powered the entire remote station.

def: A control method keeping a solar array at its most efficient operating point.
ex: {{Maximum power point tracking}} boosted the array's daily energy yield.

def: A device regulating current from panels to protect the battery.
ex: The {{charge controller}} prevented the battery from overcharging.

def: A machine that converts wind energy into rotational and electrical power.
ex: The offshore {{wind turbine}} fed clean power into the grid.

def: A group of wind turbines installed together to generate bulk electricity.
ex: The coastal {{wind farm}} supplied thousands of homes.

def: The housing atop a wind tower containing the generator and gearbox.
ex: Technicians climbed into the {{nacelle}} to service the gearbox.

ipa: /ЋИd Мbli f…Ыd …™nЋИd Мk Г…Щn ЋИd Т…Ыn…Щre…™t…Щ/
def: A wind generator whose rotor and stator both exchange power with the grid.
ex: The turbine's {{doubly-fed induction generator}} handled variable wind speeds.

def: A system that stores electrical energy in batteries for later use.
ex: The {{battery storage}} released power when demand peaked at night.

def: Equipment that stores energy and returns it to the grid on demand.
ex: The {{energy storage system}} smoothed out fluctuations in solar output.

def: The remaining capacity of a battery expressed as a percentage.
ex: The monitor showed the pack's {{state of charge}} at eighty percent.

def: The link between a generator and the public electricity network.
ex: The plant awaited approval for its {{grid connection}}.

def: An electricity network using digital control to balance supply and demand.
ex: The {{smart grid}} rerouted power automatically during the outage.

def: A small local grid able to operate independently from the main network.
ex: The island's {{microgrid}} ran on solar and battery power.

def: A backup device that supplies power instantly when the mains fail.
ex: The {{uninterruptible power supply}} kept the servers running during the blackout.

def: The branch of engineering dealing with control and conversion of electric power.
ex: Modern {{power electronics}} made compact, efficient chargers possible.

def: A semiconductor device designed to switch or control high power levels.
ex: Each {{power semiconductor}} dissipated heat through a large sink.

def: A circuit that supplies the switching signal to a power transistor's gate.
ex: A robust {{gate driver}} ensured clean turn-on of the IGBT.

def: A group of capacitors used to correct power factor or store energy.
ex: The {{capacitor bank}} improved the factory's power factor.

def: The practice of improving the ratio of real to apparent power.
ex: {{Power factor correction}} reduced the plant's electricity charges.

def: Deviation of a waveform caused by unwanted multiples of the fundamental frequency.
ex: Nonlinear loads introduced heavy {{harmonic distortion}} into the supply.

def: A measure summing all harmonic components relative to the fundamental.
ex: The filter cut the {{total harmonic distortion}} below five percent.

def: A powered circuit that dynamically cancels harmonics or reactive current.
ex: An {{active filter}} injected opposing currents to clean the waveform.

def: An inductor placed in series to limit current surges and harmonics.
ex: A {{line reactor}} protected the drive from voltage transients.

def: A device controlling motor speed by varying supply frequency and voltage.
ex: The pump's {{variable frequency drive}} saved energy at low demand.

def: A device that gradually ramps voltage to reduce motor inrush current.
ex: The {{soft starter}} eased the large motor up to full speed.

def: The high initial current drawn when equipment is first energised.
ex: The transformer's {{inrush current}} briefly tripped the breaker.

def: The fraction of a switching period during which the switch conducts.
ex: Increasing the {{duty ratio}} raised the converter's output voltage.

def: The small residual AC variation left after rectification and filtering.
ex: A larger capacitor reduced the output {{ripple voltage}}.

def: A capacitor that filters out ripple to give steadier DC output.
ex: The {{smoothing capacitor}} flattened the rectified waveform.

def: A four-diode arrangement converting AC to full-wave rectified DC.
ex: The power supply began with a {{bridge rectifier}} stage.

def: A four-switch circuit that can drive current in either direction.
ex: The {{H-bridge}} let the controller reverse the motor's direction.

def: A six-device converter handling three-phase AC to DC conversion.
ex: The industrial drive relied on a {{three-phase bridge}} rectifier.

def: An advanced technique for controlling inverter output using rotating vectors.
ex: {{Space vector modulation}} improved the inverter's voltage utilisation.

def: A motor control method decoupling torque and flux for precise response.
ex: {{Field-oriented control}} gave the servo smooth, accurate torque.

def: A method that returns a motor's braking energy back to the supply.
ex: {{Regenerative braking}} recovered energy each time the train slowed.

ipa: /ЋИbr М Гl…Щs ЋМdiЋР siЋР ЋИm…Щ Кt…Щ/
def: An electronically commutated DC motor without mechanical brushes.
ex: The drone used a compact {{brushless DC motor}}.

def: A magnet retaining its field without external current, used in rotors.
ex: The generator's rotor carried a strong {{permanent magnet}}.

def: A device generating electricity from a chemical reaction of fuel.
ex: The hydrogen {{fuel cell}} produced power with only water as waste.

def: Electricity generated from the energy of moving or falling water.
ex: The dam supplied {{hydroelectric power}} to the whole region.

def: Energy extracted from heat stored beneath the Earth's surface.
ex: The plant tapped {{geothermal energy}} from deep underground.

def: Energy from naturally replenished sources like sun, wind, and water.
ex: The country invested heavily in {{renewable energy}} projects.

def: The oscillation rate of the AC supply, nominally fifty or sixty hertz.
ex: Operators kept the {{grid frequency}} stable at fifty hertz.

def: The process of keeping grid frequency near its nominal value.
ex: Batteries provided fast {{frequency regulation}} during demand spikes.

def: The highest level of electricity consumption during a given period.
ex: Storage plants discharge to meet the evening {{peak demand}}.

def: The minimum continuous electricity demand a system must always meet.
ex: Nuclear plants supplied the steady {{base load}} around the clock.

def: Adjusting consumer power use in response to grid conditions or prices.
ex: A {{demand response}} programme cut factory loads during shortages.

def: A billing scheme crediting owners for surplus solar power exported.
ex: Under {{net metering}}, exported solar energy lowered their bill.

def: The degree to which voltage and frequency stay within ideal limits.
ex: Poor {{power quality}} damaged the sensitive laboratory equipment.

def: A brief drop in voltage below its normal operating level.
ex: A {{voltage sag}} caused the motors to stall momentarily.

def: A short-term rise in voltage above its nominal level.
ex: A {{voltage swell}} followed the sudden loss of a large load.

def: Visible variation in lamp brightness caused by voltage fluctuations.
ex: Arc furnaces produced noticeable {{flicker}} on nearby lighting.

def: A device that rapidly injects or absorbs reactive power for stability.
ex: The {{static VAR compensator}} steadied voltage at the substation.

ipa: /ЋМe…™t Г viЋР diЋР ЋИsiЋР tr√¶nzЋИm…™ Г…Щn/
def: Long-distance power transfer using high-voltage direct current.
ex: {{HVDC transmission}} linked the offshore wind farm to shore.

def: A transformer that raises voltage from primary to secondary winding.
ex: A {{step-up transformer}} boosted output for long-distance transmission.

def: A transformer that lowers voltage from primary to secondary winding.
ex: A {{step-down transformer}} supplied safe voltage to the homes.

def: A transformer mechanism adjusting winding taps to regulate voltage.
ex: The {{tap changer}} corrected the secondary voltage under load.

def: A transistor whose control gate is electrically isolated from the channel.
ex: The converter switched using an {{insulated-gate transistor}}.

def: The removal of waste heat generated by electrical components.
ex: Good {{heat dissipation}} kept the transistors within safe limits.

def: A destructive cycle where rising temperature causes ever-higher current.
ex: Poor cooling led the battery cell into {{thermal runaway}}.

def: When a generator keeps supplying a disconnected part of the grid.
ex: Anti-{{islanding}} protection shut the inverter down during outages.

def: Matching a generator's voltage, phase, and frequency to the grid.
ex: {{Synchronisation}} was confirmed before closing the breaker.

def: Deliberate reduction of renewable output when the grid cannot absorb it.
ex: Grid congestion forced {{curtailment}} of the wind farm's output.

def: The ratio of actual output to maximum possible output over time.
ex: Offshore turbines achieved a high {{capacity factor}}.

def: The power of sunlight received per unit area on a surface.
ex: Higher {{solar irradiance}} at noon boosted the array's output.

def: handheld instrument that measures voltage, current and resistance in one device
ex: He used a {{multimeter}} to check the battery voltage.

def: instrument connected in parallel to measure voltage across a component
ex: The {{voltmeter}} showed exactly twelve volts.

def: instrument connected in series to measure the current flowing in a circuit
ex: Connect the {{ammeter}} in series with the load.

def: instrument that measures the electrical power consumed by a circuit
ex: The {{wattmeter}} recorded the motor's power draw.

def: instrument used to measure electrical resistance in ohms
ex: Use an {{ohmmeter}} to check the resistor's value.

def: sensitive instrument that detects and measures small electric currents
ex: The {{galvanometer}} deflected when current passed through.

def: meter with jaws that measures current without breaking the circuit
ex: A {{clamp meter}} reads current around a live wire.

def: instrument that measures the rotational speed of a shaft or motor
ex: The {{tachometer}} displayed three thousand revolutions per minute.

def: instrument measuring air or gas flow speed, often for cooling checks
ex: An {{anemometer}} verified airflow through the cabinet.

def: device that measures the rate of fluid or gas passing through
ex: The {{flow meter}} logged coolant flow to the transformer.

def: device that records sensor measurements over time for later analysis
ex: The {{data logger}} captured temperature every ten seconds.

def: instrument producing test waveforms of controlled frequency and amplitude
ex: The {{signal generator}} fed a sine wave into the amplifier.

def: instrument generating sine, square and triangular test waveforms
ex: Set the {{function generator}} to a one-kilohertz square wave.

def: instrument that captures and displays multiple digital signals simultaneously
ex: A {{logic analyser}} traced the bus timing errors.

def: process of adjusting an instrument to match a known reference standard
ex: The meter's {{calibration}} was checked against a reference source.

def: how closely a measurement agrees with the true accepted value
ex: The instrument's {{accuracy}} is within one percent.

def: how closely repeated measurements of the same quantity agree
ex: High {{precision}} means the readings cluster tightly together.

def: allowable deviation of a value from its nominal specification
ex: The resistor has a five-percent {{tolerance}}.

def: smallest change in a quantity that an instrument can detect
ex: The meter's {{resolution}} is one millivolt.

def: ratio of output change to the input change causing it
ex: The sensor's high {{sensitivity}} detects tiny pressure changes.

def: ability of an instrument to give the same reading under identical conditions
ex: Good {{repeatability}} means consistent results across trials.

def: degree to which output is directly proportional to the input
ex: The amplifier's {{linearity}} keeps distortion very low.

def: lag where output depends on the direction of input change
ex: Magnetic {{hysteresis}} causes energy loss in the core.

def: slow unwanted change in an instrument's reading over time
ex: Thermal {{drift}} shifted the sensor's zero point.

def: constant deviation of a reading from the true value at zero
ex: An {{offset error}} added two millivolts to every reading.

def: non-zero reading shown when the true input is zero
ex: Correct the {{zero error}} before taking measurements.

def: input range over which no output change is produced
ex: The controller's {{dead band}} prevents constant switching.

def: difference between the maximum and minimum values of a range
ex: The sensor's {{span}} runs from zero to ten bar.

def: maximum reading an analogue meter's pointer can indicate
ex: At {{full-scale deflection}} the needle reads one hundred.

def: ratio of a waveform's peak value to its RMS value
ex: A high {{crest factor}} indicates sharp signal peaks.

def: time a signal takes to rise between ten and ninety percent
ex: The pulse had a very short {{rise time}}.

def: time for a signal to settle within a tolerance of its final value
ex: The amplifier's {{settling time}} is under a microsecond.

def: effective value of an alternating quantity equal to equivalent DC heating
ex: Mains voltage is quoted as a {{root mean square}} value.

def: maximum instantaneous voltage reached during one cycle of a waveform
ex: The {{peak voltage}} exceeds the RMS value.

def: total voltage difference between a waveform's highest and lowest points
ex: The {{peak-to-peak voltage}} measured twenty volts.

def: sensor whose resistance changes when it is stretched or compressed
ex: A {{strain gauge}} measured the beam's deformation.

def: sensor of two dissimilar metals producing a voltage from temperature difference
ex: The {{thermocouple}} measured the furnace temperature.

def: circuit of four resistors used to measure unknown resistance precisely
ex: A {{wheatstone bridge}} balanced the strain-gauge circuit.

def: sensor that converts a mechanical force or weight into an electrical signal
ex: The {{load cell}} weighed the crane's cargo.

def: sensor that measures acceleration or vibration of a body
ex: An {{accelerometer}} monitored the motor's vibration.

def: sensor producing voltage in response to a magnetic field
ex: A {{Hall effect sensor}} detected the shaft position.

def: device that converts fluid or gas pressure into an electrical signal
ex: The {{pressure sensor}} reported the pipeline pressure.

def: low-value resistor used to measure current by the voltage across it
ex: The {{shunt resistor}} let the meter read high currents.

def: transformer producing a scaled current for safe measurement of large currents
ex: A {{current transformer}} feeds the protection relay.

def: transformer scaling high voltage down for safe measurement
ex: The {{voltage transformer}} steps grid voltage down for the meter.

def: pointed lead used to make contact with a circuit under test
ex: Touch the {{test probe}} to the terminal.

def: safety device that disconnects supply when leakage current is detected
ex: The {{residual current device}} tripped when insulation failed.

def: maximum current a fuse carries before it melts and breaks
ex: Choose a {{fuse rating}} above the normal load current.

def: metal rod driven into soil to provide a connection to earth
ex: The {{earthing electrode}} was buried two metres deep.

def: explosive release of energy from a fault arcing across conductors
ex: An {{arc flash}} can cause severe burns instantly.

def: safety procedure that isolates and marks equipment before maintenance
ex: Follow {{lockout tagout}} before servicing the panel.

def: small unwanted current flowing where it should not, often to earth
ex: High {{leakage current}} suggests failing insulation.

def: unintended connection between a live conductor and earth
ex: An {{earth fault}} tripped the protection system.

def: voltage a person may contact between exposed metal and the ground
ex: Bonding limits the dangerous {{touch voltage}} during a fault.

def: voltage between a person's feet caused by ground current spread
ex: {{Step voltage}} near a fault can be lethal.

def: total impedance of the fault current path in an installation
ex: Measure the earth {{loop impedance}} to verify safety.

def: maximum current that would flow during a short circuit fault
ex: The {{prospective fault current}} determines the breaker rating.

def: largest fault current a breaker can safely interrupt
ex: The breaker's {{breaking capacity}} must exceed the fault level.

def: device that diverts transient voltage spikes safely to earth
ex: A {{surge protection device}} shields equipment from lightning.

def: gear worn to protect a worker from electrical and other hazards
ex: Wear the correct {{personal protective equipment}} near live parts.

def: connecting metal parts so they stay at the same voltage
ex: {{Equipotential bonding}} removes dangerous voltage differences.

def: conductor providing a safe path for fault current to earth
ex: The green-yellow wire is the {{protective conductor}}.

def: two independent insulation layers protecting a user without an earth connection
ex: The drill has {{double insulation}}, so no earth is needed.

def: code stating an enclosure's protection against dust and water
ex: An {{IP rating}} of sixty-seven means dust-tight and waterproof.

def: maximum electric field an insulator withstands before it breaks down
ex: Oil raises the transformer's {{dielectric strength}}.

def: voltage at which an insulator suddenly starts to conduct
ex: Exceeding the {{breakdown voltage}} destroys the dielectric.

def: unwanted electrical discharge arcing over an insulator's surface
ex: Rain caused a {{flashover}} across the insulator.

def: shortest path along a surface between two conductive parts
ex: A longer {{creepage distance}} reduces surface tracking.

def: formal document authorising specified work under controlled safe conditions
ex: No one starts until the {{permit to work}} is signed.

def: systematic evaluation of hazards and the controls needed to reduce them
ex: Complete a {{risk assessment}} before any high-voltage work.

def: to remove all electrical power from equipment before working on it
ex: Always {{de-energise}} the circuit before maintenance.

def: resistance between an earthing electrode and the general mass of earth
ex: Low {{earth resistance}} ensures a safe fault path.
